title-group.article-title: Neither rats nor mice have a broad thermoneutral zone: implications for physiological studies

contrib-group.aff: 1https://ror.org/0435rc536grid.425956.90000 0004 0391 2646Diabetes, Obesity and MASH, Research and Early Development, Novo Nordisk A/S, Denmark
2https://ror.org/05ggn0a85grid.448072.d0000 0004 0635 6059Department of Biochemistry and Microbiology, University of Chemistry and Technology, Prague, Czech Republic
3https://ror.org/036zr1b90grid.418930.70000 0001 2299 1368Centre for Experimental Medicine, Institute for Clinical and Experimental Medicine, Prague, Czech Republic
4https://ror.org/00adh9b73grid.419635.c0000 0001 2203 7304Diabetes, Endocrinology, and Obesity Branch, National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases NIH, Bethesda, MD USA

You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

abstract.p: Unlike humans, small mammals dedicate considerable energy expenditure (EE) to maintaining core body temperature (Tb). We investigate the influence of ambient temperature (Ta) on energy homeostasis in single housed male mice and rats studied by indirect calorimetry and continuous Tb measurement at Ta ranging from 22 °C to 35 °C. With Tb increases at higher Ta, food intake and body weight decrease, demonstrating that the higher Tb signifies heat stress and not a benign adaptive response. Rats at 30 °C were at the edge of their thermal tolerance. Neither rats nor mice have a broad thermoneutral zone (TNZ). Rather, both have thermoneutral points (TNP), below which EE increases and above which Tb increases. In mice, the EE increase above the TNP is due to the higher Tb (van’t Hoff Q10 effect) and increased physical activity. Interestingly, the remaining EE is lower, demonstrating acute thermal adaptation with a reduced basal metabolic rate. In conclusion, rats are less cold-sensitive and more prone to overheating than mice. Despite their larger size, rats, like mice, have a TNP and not a broad TNZ. Studying rats and mice at a Ta of 28–29 °C is a sensible compromise that safely minimizes cold-induced thermogenesis while reducing the risk of heat stress.

Rats and mice have a thermoneutral point below which metabolic rate rises and above which body temperature rises and heat stress occurs, with rats overheating more easily; housing temperatures of 28–29 °C best minimize both cold stress and overheating

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Mice and rats are used to model human physiology and pathophysiology and are typically the first species chosen for in vivo studies in drug development1,2. Yet, they differ from humans in several ways, with size (≈3000-fold smaller for mice and ≈300-fold smaller for rats) being a very important factor3. The geometric consequence of a smaller body is a larger surface area to weight ratio (≈14-fold greater for mice and ≈7-fold greater for rats)4 which increases sensitivity to the ambient temperature (Ta) due to greater heat loss5. To defend body temperature (Tb), small mammals use non-shivering thermogenesis, largely by activation of brown-adipose tissue (BAT), to increase heat production6. When kept at room temperature (~22 °C), single-housed mice devote ~1/3 of their total energy expenditure (TEE) to maintaining Tb7,8. If challenged (e.g., by fasting or cold exposure), mice can thermoregulate to a slightly reduced Tb8 or more profoundly with torpor9, conserving energy and reducing nutritional needs. Rat temperature sensitivity has also been studied10. As expected from their greater body size they are less sensitive to a cool environment than mice, with a wide range of required EE reported to maintain Tb at room temperature10–12.

Central to the subject is the concept of the thermoneutral zone (TNZ)13,14. The TNZ is formally defined as “the Ta range in which temperature regulation is achieved only by control of sensible heat loss, i.e., without regulatory changes in metabolic heat production or evaporative heat loss”15,16, often simplified as the Ta range over which the resting metabolic rate is at its minimum5,6. At the lower end of the TNZ, energetically inexpensive warming mechanisms (e.g., changes in vasoconstriction, body posture, chosen environment, nest quality) are exhausted and below this point increased EE must be used to maintain body core temperature (Tb). A variety of TNZ ranges have been reported for single housed mice, with a mean span of 2 °C and median range of 30–32 °C17 see also5). The reported TNZ for single housed rats varies widely between studies (e.g., 28–32 °C in one study18 and ranging from a lower bound of 22–30 °C to an upper bound of 27–34 °C across studies11). We have proposed an update to the formal definition of the TNZ, additionally requiring that Tb does not increase as this signifies stress and not a benign adaptive response19.

While mice and rats are often studied below thermoneutrality, humans live at thermoneutrality. Due to these differences in thermal biology, it has been argued that increasing rodent housing temperature may increase translatability of rodent data to humans and reduce the number of failed clinical drug trials20–23. However, the optimal housing temperature to achieve this for mice is the subject of debate24,25 and little data are available for rats. For development of weight management drugs, conventional wisdom has been that thermoneutral-like conditions should improve the likelihood of translatability of results to humans. However, comparing weight loss efficacy in mice at Ta 22 °C and 30 °C to human clinical results does not support this26.

To better understand and guide optimal housing temperatures for metabolic studies, we investigated Ta’s from 22 °C to 35 °C. Rats tolerate a high Ta more poorly than mice. We also found that rats, like mice, do not have a broad TNZ, but rather have a thermoneutral point (TNP). Studying rats and mice at a Ta of 28–29 °C safely minimizes cold-induced thermogenesis.

sec.title: Ethical considerations
sec.p: Animal studies were approved by Danish Animal Experiments Inspectorate (2020-15-0201-00683) in accordance with the National Institutes of Health Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory (publication number 85-23) and the European Convention for the Protection of Vertebrate Animals used for Experimental and other Scientific Purposes (Council of Europe No 123, Strasbourg 1985). We have complied with all relevant ethical regulations for animal use.

sec.title: Animals, housing, and pre-experimental procedures
sec.p: Chow-fed C57BL/6 J male mice were sourced from Charles River (Germany, supply code: C57BL/6 J [N] Charles River, Germany, CRL DE) and were 20–25 g and 11 weeks at arrival. Mice were housed in groups of 10 for 11 days and then were housed singly.

DIO (diet induced obese) C57BL/6 J male mice were sourced from Charles River (France, supply code: CH7BLP/6 J, DIO 60% HFD [N], Charles River France, CRL FR), were 20 weeks and ~41 g at arrival. Mice were housed in groups of 5 for 15 days and then were housed singly.

Chow-fed male Sprague Dawley (SD) rats were from Janvier Labs (France, supply code: Sprague Dawley [N], Janvier Labs, France, JAN, FR) and were 13 weeks and ~250 g at arrival. Rats were housed in groups for 4 for 10 days and then were housed singly.

DIO male SD rats were obtained from Charles River (France, supply code: Sprague Dawley, DIO 60% HFD [N] Charles River, France CRL, FR). Rats were 22 weeks at arrival and were single housed on arrival. In all cases animals were housed at Ta ≈22 °C under standard conditions that included bedding, a house, chew stick, and nesting material and followed a 12:12 light:dark cycle with lights on at 0600 h. Chow animals were fed ad libitum Altromin (cat. no. 1324, Brogaarden, DK; 3.227 kcal/kg) and DIO animals ad libitum 45% high fat diet (Cat. no. D12451, Research Diets, NJ, USA; 4.73 kcal/kg). Cohort baseline characteristics, including age at study start, are in Table 1. Body composition was determined (Echo MRI, TX, USA) before, but not during, indirect calorimetry, avoiding any interference with the metabolic measurements. For mice, scans were performed before implantation of the Tb measuring capsule as they interfered with the measurements.Table 1
Cohort characteristics at baseline
ChowDIOP-value
Mice
n811
Body weight (g)26.5 ± 0.2842.9 ± 0.96P < 0.0001
Age (wk)1522
Fat mass (g)3.19 ± 0.7812.3 ± 0.99P < 0.0001
Fat free mass (g)24.0 ± 0.3827.7 ± 0.60P < 0.01
Fat (%)13.3 ± 2.9028.9 ± 1.60P < 0.0001
Rats
n88
Age (wk)1554
Body weight (g)496 ± 9.12899 ± 16.3P < 0.0001
Fat mass (g)45.1 ± 2.59263 ± 17.1P < 0.0001
Fat free mass (g)353 ± 11.3492 ± 12.9P < 0.0001
Fat (%)9.09 ± 1.2529.5 ± 2.08P < 0.0001
The mouse chow data are for the standard housing cohort. Data are mean ± SEM; statistical significance was assessed by unpaired t-test. DIO Diet-induced obese.

sec.title: Implantation of Tb measuring capsule
sec.p: Implantation of a temperature recording capsule (17.7 mm × 8.9 mm; 1.7 g; Anipill, Animals monitoring, Hérouville Saint-Clair, France)27 was done under sterile conditions under isoflurane anesthesia, starting 30 min after pretreatment with antibiotics and analgesics (Rimadyl: 5 mg/kg sc, Baytril: 10 mg/kg sc, and Anorfin: 0.05 mg/kg sc). Implantation was done with a midline incision (≈2 cm) and after capsule insertion the muscle layer was closed with interrupted sutures and the skin was closed with running hidden sutures. Animals were single-housed after surgery, treated for three days with Rimadyl (5 mg/kg, s.c., daily), monitored daily, and studied after at least a week of recovery.

sec.title: Measurement and analysis of TEE, food and water intake, activity level and Tb
sec.p: Measurement of TEE was done in a temperature adjustable indirect calorimetry system from Sable Systems International (Promethion Core System®, NV, USA). Mice and rats were single-housed at Ta 22 °C with the following enrichments: bedding material (65 g for mice and 340 g for rats), a transparent plastic house/hide, nesting material (≈8 g for mice, ≈20 g for rats), and a wooden stick chew toy (mice: S-bricks and rats: M-bricks, Tapvej Estonia Oü, Estonia) (Supplementary Fig. 1A). Cage dimensions were (l/h/w): mice: 35.5/17.5/13.5 cm, chow rats: 46/27/20 cm, and DIO rats: 46/35/20.5 cm. Flow was 2000 ml/min for chow mice, 2500 ml/min for DIO mice, 3000 ml/min for chow rats, and 4000 ml/min DIO rats. All animals followed a 12:12 h light:dark cycle with lights on at 0800 h for chow mice, DIO mice, and chow rats and at 2300 h for DIO rats (on this schedule for 7-10 days before calorimetry). Animals were acclimatized for 5–7 days in the indirect calorimetry chambers before collection of baseline data (Ta 22 °C). Ta was increased from 22 °C to 32 °C (rats) or 35 °C (mice) with 24 h at each Ta as shown in Supplementary Fig. 1B. The Ta was changed at the start of the dark phase. Mice and rats were kept on their respective diets to which they had ad libitum access. Food was replaced daily (0700–0800 h for chow mice, chow rats, and DIO mice and 0900–1000 h for DIO rats) and consumption was recorded by manual weighing. Activity was measured with infrared beam arrays (Promethion BZ1) generating distance travelled. For animal welfare reasons DIO rats were housed in larger cages, which precluded activity measurement.

Indirect calorimetry time points were obtained at 2.5 min intervals. Data were processed by Sable Systems Macro Interpreter v.2.41, filtering out technically aberrant values, binning in 5 min intervals, and calculating TEE and respiratory exchange ratio (RER) as reported26. TEE data are reported per animal, not divided by body weight or fat free mass as per guidelines28. On the last day of measurements, body composition data were obtained by magnetic resonance spectroscopy (EchoMRI, TX, USA).

In mice, defending Tb accounts for most of the daily variation in TEE because of target Tb changes due to phase (light vs dark) and arousal state (resting/sleeping vs active/awake)29. We used the TEE 5th percentile to estimate the resting/sleeping EE (“resting EE”) and the TEE 95th percentile to estimate the active/awake EE. Analyses using the 5th vs 10th percentiles gave nearly identical results, indicating that the 5th percentile robustly captures the resting/sleeping TEE. In contrast, the 95th percentile TEE was greater than the 90th percentile TEE.

sec.title: Treatment with the β3-adrenergic receptor agonist, CL-316243
sec.p: After the last (warmest) day of the Ta ramp, the mice/rats were returned to 22 °C for 48 h and then treated with the β3-adrenoceptor agonist CL-31624330,31 at the start of the light phase and again 12 h later. CL-316243 has an elimination t1/2 of ≈6 h in rats32. At 12 h after the second dose the Ta was increased to 30 °C and after 24 h at 30 °C another dose of CL-316243 was given to the mice only. Since some CL-316243 effects persisted for 12–36 h after dosing, the reference intervals for the CL-316243 treatments were the 22 °C and 30 °C days from the Ta ramp. The interval studied is the first 12 h (light phase) after the dose. For technical reasons, the 22 °C reference interval for the chow mice was from the day before the 22 °C CL-316243 treatment. The CL-316243 (Cat no. C5976 Sigma Aldrich, Brøndby, Denmark) dose for mice is 300 nmol/kg s.c. in 5 ml/kg saline and for rats, 200 nmol/kg s.c. in 0.5 ml/ kg saline.

sec.title: Calculation of physical activity energy expenditure (PAEE) and Q10 effect contributions to TEE
sec.p: Data from control C57BL/6 J male mice were collected as reported19. In brief, mice were acclimated to 22 °C in a CLAMS indirect calorimetry system (CLAMS, Columbus Instruments, Columbus, OH) and then exposed for 1 h to each Ta, with measurements of Tb, TEE, RER, total activity (single axis beam breaks, 0.5-inch inter-beam distance), and food intake taken every 13 minutes, yielding five sampling cycles per 65-minute period.

sec.title: Statistics and reproducibility
sec.p: Data are presented as mean ± SEM. Graphs were made using GraphPad Prism 10 (La Jolla, CA) and statistical significance was tested in GraphPad Prism or Microsoft Excel (Redmond, WA). Within animal differences (e.g., dark vs. light phase) were assessed by paired t-test. Between group differences were tested by unpaired t-test or one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey multiple comparison test. P < 0.05 was considered significant, based on two-tailed testing. Numbers of animals per groups are indicated in each figure or table. All experiments performed are presented.

sec.title: Reporting summary
sec.p: Further information on research design is available in the Nature Portfolio Reporting Summary linked to this article.

sec.title: Both rats and mice have TNPs, and not broad TNZs
sec.p:
We compared the effect of increasing Ta (24 h at each Ta) using housing conditions that include nesting and enrichment materials. For reference, housing male mice without nesting and enrichment increases energy use, with the magnitude depending on circadian phase and arousal state (Supplementary Fig. 1A–I). Previously we reported (with 1 h at each Ta) that mice have a thermoneutral point (TNP), a discrete Ta below which TEE increases and above which Tb increases, and not a broad TNZ19. The current chow and DIO mouse cohorts demonstrate the EE and Tb vs Ta changes characteristic of a TNP, with TNP values in approximately the 30 °C to 32 °C range (Fig. 1A, B, E, F). These mouse results demonstrate that the TNP concept applies when using different assay durations and when nesting materials are present.Fig. 1Thermal physiology and heat stress in mice and rats under standard housing conditions.
Experiments are in columns as indicated. Effect of Ta on (A–D) Tb, (E–H) resting EE, (I–L) RER, (M–O) physical activity, (P–S) food intake, and (T–W) body weight. Dotted line at Ta = 30 °C indicates the start of data not used subsequently for Scholander analysis. n = 7–11/group. Paired t-test p-values for 30 °C vs 32 °C (light, dark): A 0.015, 0.99; B 0.0013, 0.0047; C 0.0083, 0.019, D 0.0001, 0.00001. Individual Data from individual animals are in Supplementary Fig. 2A–W. The regression lines in (E–H) use all Ta; regression parameters are in Supplementary Table 1. Study days are indicated in top panels (A–D). Data are mean ± SEM.

Examining the rat data, the resting EE increased as the Ta was reduced below 32 °C, while the Tb increased above 30 °C (Fig. 1C, D, G, H). These observations in both the chow and DIO cohorts demonstrate that rats have a TNP. The results suggest that the rat TNP is approximately 30 °C, although more measurements with finer Ta increments are needed for adequate precision. Thus, despite their larger size, rats, like mice, do not have a broad TNZ but rather have a TNP.

sec.title: The Tb increases at higher Ta indicate heat stress, in both rats and mice
sec.p: In mice, Tb increased gradually with increasing Ta, starting at ≈31 °C to ≈33 °C (Fig. 1A, B). In contrast, the increase in rats was more abrupt at a Ta of 31 °C and some of the DIO rats did not tolerate a Ta of 32 °C (Fig. 1C, D). In all four cohorts the Tb increase was accompanied by reductions in food intake, RER, and body weight, without consistent changes in physical activity (Fig. 1I–W). The lower food intake going from 22 °C to 30 °C reflects the tight coupling between energy expenditure and energy intake. At Ta 30 °C, the need for cold-indued thermogenesis is minimal, causing TEE to be lower and thus food intake also to be lower. The fact that body weight does not change shows that energy intake and expenditure are indeed matched before heat stress occurs at Ta >30 °C. Above Ta 30 °C there is a reduction in food intake below steady state needs, causing use of stored fat and weight loss. Thus, the Tb increase in both mice and rats signifies heat stress and is not simply a benign adaptive response.

sec.title: Mice, but not rats, tolerate treatment with a β3-adrenergic agonist at Ta 30 °C
sec.p:
Selective β3-adrenergic agonists stimulate lipolysis in white adipose tissue and thermogenesis in BAT and have been used to investigate thermogenic capacity and heat dissipation in mice35–37. In rats at 22 °C, CL-316243 treatment was reported to increase Tb by 0.6 °C to 0.9 °C38. We found that CL-316243 treatment of chow rats generated the expected increase in Tb and TEE and reduction of RER and food intake (Fig. 2A–E). DIO rats behaved similarly at 22 °C but when exposed to 30 °C after CL-316243 treatment, the experiment had to be terminated due to heat stress with a large Tb increase (Fig. 2F–L). These results confirm that rats at 30 °C are near their thermal tolerance limit, with DIO rats unable to compensate for the heat load from β3-agonist stimulation. Mice, due to their smaller size and higher conductance, are better at dissipating extra heat production, with no clear difference between chow-fed and DIO mice after treatment with CL-316243 (Supplementary Fig. 3).Fig. 2Effect of β3-adrenoceptor agonist treatment in rats.
A Tb, (B) TEE, (C) RER, (D) activity, and (E) energy intake over 12 h (24 h for energy intake) at baseline (gray) or after CL-316243 treatment (red, 200 nmol/kg s.c.) in chow rats at the indicated Ta. F–I As above for DIO rats at Ta = 22 °C. J–L Time course of Tb, TEE, and RER at showing the effect of CL-316243 treatment (200 nmol/kg s.c., at red arrows) in DIO rats. Ta switch from 22 °C to 30 °C indicated by black arrow. Data are mean ± SEM, n = 7–8/group, P-values from paired t-tests.

sec.title: Scholander analysis of the effect of Ta on TEE provides no clear evidence for increased insulation with increased adiposity
sec.p:
Analysis of the effect of Ta on TEE from the TEE vs Ta plot using the classic approach of Scholander provides further insights into thermal biology13,14. Since TEE depends on activity status (active/awake vs resting/sleep), the TEE 5th percentile was used as a measure of the resting/sleeping metabolic rate29. For Ta below thermoneutrality, the extrapolated x intercept of the resting EE vs Ta line is the body’s Tb set point since no added energy expenditure is required to defend Tb (Fig. 3A). At Ta ≥ 30 °C the resting EE deviates from the regression line (indicating that Ta is approaching thermoneutrality), so the Ta from 22 °C to 28 °C were used for these regressions (Fig. 3A). In the mouse cohorts, the x intercepts were near the measured Tb (Fig. 3B, Table 2). However, as noted previously11, the x intercepts were much higher in the rats (46 °C to 75 °C) despite excellent fits to the regression lines.Fig. 3No evidence for increased insulation with increasing body weight.
A Annotated Scholander14 graph using the mouse chow, light phase, resting data. Regression line uses only the Ta<30 °C points. B x intercept (dotted line indicates 37 °C), (C) negative of the slope, and (D–G) body weight and fat mass dependence of the slope. Regression parameters are in Table 2. H Percentage increase in resting EE at 22 °C above resting EE at 30 °C, for the indicated cohorts and (I–L) body weight and fat mass dependence of the percentage increase. The correlations in (D–G, I–L) were not significant. Data in (B, C, I) are mean + SEM. n = 6–11/group.Table 2
Scholander plot (resting EE vs Ta) regression using Ta = 22 °C to 28 °C
Chow mouseDIO mouseChow ratDIO rat
LightDarkLightDarkLightDarkLightDark
R squared0.936 ± 0.0270.878 ± 0.0420.944 ± 0.0140.944 ± 0.0160.904 ± 0.0280.854 ± 0.0710.801 ± 0.0600.683 ± 0.090
n7611117765
Slope (kcal/h/°C)−0.014 ± 0.001−0.013 ± 0.002−0.020 ± 0.002−0.018 ± 0.001−0.052 ± 0.008−0.070 ± 0.008−0.062 ± 0.006−0.050 ± 0.012
Y-intercept (kcal/h)0.56 ± 0.030.57 ± 0.070.78 ± 0.040.79 ± 0.043.08 ± 0.223.96 ± 0.203.58 ± 0.143.69 ± 0.37
X-intercept (°C)40.3 ± 0.947.4 ± 4.240.6 ± 1.045.0 ± 1.468.6 ± 10.759.9 ± 5.359.9 ± 4.280.6 ± 7.7
Resting EE 20 °C, % above 30 °C101 ± 975 ± 16104 ± 1172 ± 735 ± 640 ± 637 ± 523 ± 6
Regression parameters for Fig. 3B, C. Regressions were performed for each individual mouse or rat. Data are mean ± SEM. For mice, data were excluded if the R2 was < 0.5 (one instance, chow dark). For rats, data were excluded if the R2 was < 0.2 (one DIO light; two DIO dark).

The slope of the resting EE vs Ta regression line is the whole-body thermal conductance, the heat loss from the animal, while the inverse of the slope is the insulation (Fig. 3A, C, Table 2). The slopes were similar in chow and DIO animals, with no clear body weight or fat mass dependence in either rats or mice (Fig. 3D−G). Since the whole-body conductance and resting EE depend on body size in complex ways39, we used the regression line percentage increase in resting EE at 22 °C vs. 30 °C as a measure of the conductance/insulation that is less dependent on body weight (Fig. 3H). The resting EE % increases in rats were 35% (chow) and 37% (DIO). The resting EE increases in mice were greater, 101% (chow) and 104% (DIO). Again, there was no body weight or fat mass dependence within the cohorts (Fig. 3I−L). We also calculated the whole-body conductance as resting EE/(Tb-Ta)40 at Ta = 22 °C and did not detect a body weight or fat mass dependence.

These results demonstrate that higher body fat does not detectably increase thermal insulation in either mice or rats. Larger datasets or different approaches (such as ex vivo measurement of pelt insulation41) are required to determine the limits of detection for differences in conductance.

sec.title: TEE increases above the mouse TNP are due to PAEE and the Q10 effect
sec.p:
In the current results, at Ta above the TNP the TEE is constant (or decreasing), while it increased in the previous study19. To understand the basis for this difference, we quantified the effect of Tb and physical activity on TEE. A rise in Tb will increase TEE as described by the van’t Hoff coefficient (Q10)34. Additionally, above thermoneutrality physical activity energy expenditure (PAEE) increases TEE because it is not offset by reductions in cold-induced thermogenesis33. Notably, transiently increased activity was observed previously with one hour at each Ta19, but not over the 24 h per Ta used in the current study. Further examining the previous data (Fig. 4A, B), we calculated the Tb and physical activity contributions to the TEE (for Ta > 33 °C, which precludes cold-induced thermogenesis). Regression of TEE, TEE-PAEE, TEE-Q10, and TEE-PAEE-Q10, revealed significant independent contributions of Tb and PA to TEE (Fig. 4C, D, Table 3). These observations were confirmed in the dark phase dataset (Fig. 4E, Table 3). Therefore, the TEE increase observed above the TNP is explained by the Tb/Q10 effect and physical activity.Fig. 4Contribution of the Tb/Q10 effect and PAEE to TEE above thermoneutrality.
A, B Body temperature and ambulatory activity as a function of Ta (from ref. 19). Data are from 1 h of observations at each Ta (n = 4–5 observations/mouse) during the light phase (mean ± SEM, n = 16–21 mice). C Light phase TEE showing the effect of adjusting for Tb and physical activity, namely TEE, TEE-PAEE, TEE corrected for the Tb increase (TEE-Q10), and TEE-PAEE-Q10. The inset shows the regression lines for all data at Ta>33 °C. D, E The regression slopes for light phase TEE, TEE-PAEE, TEE-Q10, and TEE-PAEE-Q10 at Ta>33 °C from each mouse (light: n = 16–21 mice, n = 33–42 observations/mouse; dark: n = 17 mice, n = 31 observations/mouse). Regression parameters are in Table 3. P-values from 2-way ANOVA: light phase PAEE p = 0.015, Tb/Q10 p < 0.0001, interaction p > 0.99; dark phase PAEE p = 0.027, Tb/Q10 p < 0.0001, interaction p > 0.99. F Schematic showing the light phase contributions to TEE of the PAEE, Q10, thermic effect of food (TEF, in gray), and BMR at Ta = 32.4 °C and 36.6 °C. Data are in (A–E) are mean ± SEM.Table 3
Regression parameters for TEE at Ta > 33 °C
Slope (kcal/h/°C)Y intercept (kcal/h)R squaredP-value vs 0 slope
Light phase
TEE0.0162 ± 0.0035−0.319 ± 0.0700.412 ± 0.0900.028 ± 0.006
TEE-PAEE0.0106 ± 0.0023−0.147 ± 0.0320.330 ± 0.0720.158 ± 0.034
TEE-Q10−0.0044 ± 0.00100.363 ± 0.0790.163 ± 0.0360.211 ± 0.046
TEE-PAEE-Q10−0.0100 ± 0.00220.535 ± 0.1170.384 ± 0.0840.045 ± 0.010
Dark phase
TEE0.0159 ± 0.0039−0.242 ± 0.0590.285 ± 0.0690.071 ± 0.017
TEE-PAEE0.0120 ± 0.0029−0.142 ± 0.0340.270 ± 0.0650.106 ± 0.026
TEE-Q10−0.0038 ± 0.00090.413 ± 0.1000.070 ± 0.0170.308 ± 0.075
TEE-PAEE-Q10−0.0077 ± 0.00190.514 ± 0.1250.222 ± 0.0540.191 ± 0.046
Top section is light phase, n = 21 mice, see Fig. 4A–D. Bottom section is dark phase, n = 17 mice, see Fig. 4E. Data are mean ± SEM.

Quantifying the TEE components in the light phase at the highest Ta tested, 36.6 °C, PAEE contributed 11%, the Tb/Q10 effect contributed 27%, and the thermic effect of food (TEF) contributed 1.6%, leaving the BMR as 61% (Fig. 4F). For comparison, at 32.4 °C the PAEE was 3%, TEF 1.7%, and BMR 96%. Notably the BMR at 36.6 °C was reduced by 18% compared to the BMR at 32.4 °C. Thus, even with only 1 h at each temperature and in an experiment specifically designed to minimize thermal acclimation, the mice were able to acutely reduce their BMR in an adaptative response to a hot environment.

sec.title: Ta effects on energy physiology
sec.p: Increasing housing temperature to minimize cold-induced thermogenesis is postulated to better align mouse and human thermal biology22,23,42. Whether improved translatability can be obtained by increasing Ta in rats is unknown. Here we compared rats to mice and obtained results consistent with the principles of heat transfer, namely that smaller bodies (mice) lose heat more readily and are relatively cold sensitive, while larger bodies (rats) dissipate heat less well and tend more towards heat intolerance. Our studies used housing conditions that include nesting and enrichment materials, as recommended for research animals43. In mice, there was a ≈100% increase in resting TEE at 22 °C compared to 30 °C while the increase in rats was only ≈35%. Thus, below thermoneutrality, the rat is much less sensitive to reductions in Ta than is the mouse.

At warm Ta, mice gradually increased their Tb. In contrast, the increase in rats was more abrupt, steeper, and less well tolerated, especially in DIO rats. Rats showed heat stress starting at 31 °C, and BAT activation with a β3-adrenergic agonist was not tolerated in DIO rats at Ta 30 °C.

sec.title: Rats and mice have a thermoneutral point and not a broad thermoneutral zone
sec.p: The mouse results are an independent confirmation of the TNP concept. We show here that rats also have a TNP and not a broad TNZ. These results and scaling principles suggest that a TNP, not a TNZ, is the generally expected thermal physiology in small homeotherms. This is in fundamental contrast to humans who have a broad TNZ spanning ≈10–16 °C (reviewed in ref. 19).

The TEE increase above the TNP is due to a combination of Q10 effect (from the Tb increase) and PAEE that is not offset by a reduction in cold-induced thermogenesis. Indeed, above the TNP and after removal of the Tb/Q10 and PAEE contributions, the TEE is actually decreasing with increasing Ta (Fig. 4C). This means that the mice reduced their basal energy expenditure to counter as much of the Tb increase as possible.

Above the TNP, the Q10 effect contributes more to the TEE increase than does PAEE. This result supports using an increase in Tb as definition of the upper border of thermoneutrality as the Tb is the main mechanistic driver of the TEE increase. Indeed, while the reference definition of the TNZ does not include Tb15,16, some have assumed that Tb is constant in their discussion of the TNZ5.

sec.title: Choosing a Ta for rat and mouse studies
sec.p: The optimal Ta for studying mice is debated, particularly for studying weight loss pharmacotherapies. One common strategy suggests 30 °C as thermoneutrality 25. Another proposed a few degrees below thermoneutrality 24. However, mice do not have a broad TNZ, but rather a TNP that changes diurnally19 and probably also between the rest/sleep and active/awake states29. Thus, housing strictly at thermoneutrality is simply not feasible. When trying to minimize cold-induced thermogenesis, a Ta just below thermoneutrality has multiple attributes in its favor: (1) it minimizes the energy spent on cold-induced thermogenesis and the food intake needed to fuel it, mimicking the physiology of larger homeotherms8, (2) it avoids increasing Tb, which stimulates inflammation and immune responses44, worsens metabolic fitness45, and may contribute to reduced lifespan46, and (3) it provides a safety margin for variation in vivarium temperature. For mice, a Ta of ≈28–29 °C fulfills these criteria19.

An optimal Ta for minimizing cold-induced thermogenesis in rats has not been reported. Applying the criteria above to the data reported herein leads to suggesting a Ta of ≈28–29 °C for rats also. It is particularly important to avoid Ta≥30 °C for rats to reduce the risk of heat stress, which is why we suggest 29 °C as an upper bound.

A fundamental consideration when choosing the Ta is the purpose of the experiment, particularly for thermal physiology endpoints. For example, acutely transferring a mouse to 30 °C followed by maximal β3-adrenergic agonist stimulation allows quantification of the animal’s thermogenic capacity, allowing detection of subtle differences37,47. However, to completely remove cold-induced thermogenesis for measurement of the cost of physical activity, a Ta of 35 °C was required33. Generally, group housing48–51 and nesting materials52,53, both of which reduce the thermal demand, are avoided. However, huddling and nesting allow animals to manipulate their thermal environment, thereby reducing the importance of Ta choice by the experimenter. Sometimes studies at two Ta, such as ≈22 °C and ≈28–29 °C will prove informative.

Since the TEE vs Ta slope is shallower in rats, the choice of Ta is probably less crucial and ≈22 °C could be a reasonable selection for most experiments. An argument can also be made for allowing group housing with nesting materials at ≈22 °C, even when studying obesity drug efficacy. The bottom line is that the Ta should be chosen based on the experimental goals.

sec.title: Fat does not provide major thermal insulation in rats or mice
sec.p: In larger mammals such as humans, increasing adiposity contributes to thermal insulation54,55, although it is at best, a minor effect in mice7,8 where fur is the main insulating factor providing 30–50% of the total insulation7. Factors contributing to the minimal insulation by adipose tissue in mice include: (1) fat distribution—the subcutaneous adipose tissue mainly in shoulder and inguinal regions and not particularly surrounding the body core39, (2) the low absolute amount of fat—Newton’s law of heat transfer states that insulation is proportional to the layer’s thickness, and mouse adipose tissue is only millimeters thick40, and (3) mice are so small that little heat is conserved via redistribution of blood flow away from the paws and tail56 — the body core is simply too close to the body surface. We show here that adiposity, while more important in rats than in mice due to the greater fat thickness and larger body size, is still not a major contributor to insulation.

sec.title: Acute and chronic adaptation to environmental temperatures
sec.p: Physiologic adaptation to temperature extremes can mean the difference between life and death. In humans and mice, chronic adaptation to a hot environment occurs over days to weeks57,58. Here we report a BMR reduction of 18% in mice exposed to a hot Ta over a span of minutes, demonstrating that mice can acutely adapt to the heat. The BMR reduction counters the TEE increase caused by the hyperthermic Tb/Q10 effect but the underlying mechanisms for the BMR reduction are not known. Possible contributors include reduced sympathetic tone and heart rate and decreased perfusion of and metabolism by the gastrointestinal tract.

Whether there is a TNP or a broad TNZ likely depends on the body size of the animals/species studied and not the duration of exposure to a Ta. Indeed, when TEE is measured at Ta = 22 °C and Ta = 30 °C, the absolutes levels and the 22 °C:30 °C ratios are similar whether the mice have been at these Ta for 4 weeks26, one hour19, or one day, as in the current study.

sec.title: Study limitations
sec.p: We studied male rats and mice, one strain each, under single-housing conditions for 24 h at each Ta. The results may be influenced by environment considerations including the type and amount of bedding, nesting, and enrichment materials and by animal characteristic including age, strain, coat condition, and fur density. There are known sex differences in thermal biology for single housed mice59,60 and particularly with group housing, since females huddle more than males48–51,61. While single housing may be more stressful than group housing, it is required for using a Scholander analysis59. With prolonged exposure to hot or cold temperatures, mice and rats initiate adaptation mechanisms, including increasing fur density to improve insulation and behavioral changes such spreading of saliva to increase evaporative heat loss62,63. Such adaptions are more important the farther one is from the thermoneutrality. Since our study was performed within a relative narrow range near the TNP, we expect that the Ta:TEE relationships we report will be generally applicable, including in chronic studies. We have studied only mice and rats, but since body size drives the physiology, we expect that the principles reported herein apply generally to other similarly small mammals.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: The results align with the principles of heat transfer, namely that due to their smaller body size, mice lose heat readily and tend towards cold sensitivity. Despite their larger size, rats do not differ considerably from mice in thermal biology and have a TNP instead of a broad TNZ as humans. However, compared to mice, rats tend towards heat intolerance. To minimize the inter-species gap in thermal biology between humans and mice and rats, we suggest safely minimizing cold-induced thermogenesis by using a Ta of ≈28–29 °C.

fn.p: Publisher’s note Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

sec.title: Supplementary information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1038/s42003-026-09534-w.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: This study was sponsored by Novo Nordisk A/S and in part (MLR) by the Intramural Research Program of the National Institutes of Health, National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases (NIDDK) within the National Institutes of Health (NIH) (ZIA DK075062) and the National Institute for Research of Metabolic and Cardiovascular Diseases (Programme EXCELES, Project No. LX22NPO5104, Funded by the European Union—Next Generation EU) and (VS, MV) Specific University Research – Grant No. A1_FPBT_2025_001 and grant No A2_FPBT_2025_005. The contributions of the NIH author(s) were made as part of their official duties as NIH federal employees, are in compliance with agency policy requirements, and are considered Works of the United States Government. However, the findings and conclusions presented in this paper are those of the author(s) and do not necessarily reflect the views of the NIH or the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services. The authors would like to express their gratitude to the technicians in our animal unit at Novo Nordisk (Måløv, Denmark) for daily caretaking and Oksana Gavrilova and Kong Chen for helpful discussions.

notes.title: Author contributions
notes.p: J.M.J., M.L.R., and R.E.K. conceptualized the study. J.M.J., K.P., M.V., and V.Š. performed experiments. J.M.J., K.P., M.V., V.Š., M.L.R., and R.E.K. analysed and interpreted data. MLR and REK drafted the manuscript; J.M.J., K.P., and V.Š. critically revised it and provided important intellectual content.

sec.title: Peer review information
sec.p: Communications Biology thanks the anonymous reviewers for their contribution to the peer review of this work. Primary Handling Editors: Rosie Bunton-Stasyshyn & Johannes Stortz. A peer review file is available.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: Numerical source data for graphs and tables in main figures can be accessed at 10.6084/m9.figshare.30401317 (https://figshare.com/articles/dataset/_b_Neither_rats_nor_mice_have_a_broad_thermoneutral_zone_Implications_for_physiological_studies_b_/30401317?file=59045903)64. All other data are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: J.M.J., K.P., and R.E.K. declare that they are employed by Novo Nordisk A/S and K.P. is a minor shareholder. All other authors declare no competing interests.
